updates.summary: Oliver County Zoning Board proposed a 3-year moratorium on data centers by a 4-3 vote, later adopted by the county commission on December 12, 2024. The moratorium covers all data center and cryptocurrency mining facilities in the county while zoning standards are developed. The action was prompted by an unnamed company expressing interest in developing a data center project in the county. A county commissioner received an invitation to tour Applied Digital's facility in Ellendale but could not confirm that company was the developer.

Sierra Club Toiyabe Chapter appealed the Planning Commission's approval of Webb Data Center's 82,000 sq ft facility, arguing the city established zoning guidelines without public input. The City Council denied the appeal. Commissioner Becerra advocated for a comprehensive data center ordinance. Former City Manager Doug Thornley registered as a lobbyist for the data center project, raising conflict of interest concerns.

Copia Power Devco LLC (backed by Carlyle) received 4-1 approval from Lyon County commissioners for a Master Plan Amendment to build the Monarch Data Center - 4.6 million sq ft across 14 buildings on 505 acres in Mason Valley, Yerington. The 1,000MW facility would require enough electricity to power roughly 500,000 homes. Multiple Change.org petitions oppose the project, citing water scarcity in the drought-prone region, corruption allegations against officials, and impacts on the agricultural community. Commissioner Hockaday cast the lone dissenting vote.

Millard County Commission unanimously rezoned 1,100+ acres from agricultural to heavy industrial for Creekstone Energy's 10GW data center campus despite significant public opposition. The county is under 100% drought conditions per NOAA. Residents argue the rezone permanently shifts the area's rural character and threatens wildlife. Large data centers in the area could use up to 5 million gallons of water per day. Creekstone and Joule Power claim closed-loop cooling and water recycling systems will achieve 90% water reduction, but residents remain skeptical.

B+F Timpanogos proposed converting a warehouse in Provo's East Bay into a 66,000 sq ft, 30MW data center on 9 acres. Mayor Marsha Judkins formally opposed the project, saying an additional data center would not be in residents' best interest. Despite the mayor's opposition, the Planning Commission recommended rezoning approval 5-2 and unanimously approved the project plan. Concerns focused on water usage, the facility's proximity to Rocky Mountain University, and whether approximately 30 jobs would justify the water and power consumption.

Applied Digital's $7B data center campus in the 1,000-person town of Ellendale spans approximately 900 contracted acres with a 250MW initial capacity and a $5B, 15-year lease with CoreWeave. The data center uses a closed-loop cooling system. Community concerns centered on water use, power costs, noise, increased crime, and potential brownouts. Applied Digital engaged in community outreach as part of planning, but residents remain concerned about long-term impacts.

Applied Digital's $3B Polaris Forge 2 data center (280MW) proposed near the 900-person town of Harwood on approximately 900 acres sparked a confrontational community meeting on August 25, 2025. Residents peppered the company VP with questions for 90 minutes, raising concerns about noise, utility rate increases, sprawl, and lack of transparency (NDAs required for council members). Harwood approved the project in September and construction began shortly after. The project triggered an annexation battle with the City of Fargo, which sought to annex the site for tax revenue, though Fargo later withdrew its annexation attempts.

County Commission advanced 4-1 preliminary approval for BorderPlex Digital Assets' $165B Project Jupiter data center near Santa Teresa on approximately 1,400 acres. The project was initially a $50B proposal that grew to $165B total. Each data center building is projected to use approximately 625,000 gallons of water per day via closed-loop cooling. The Empowerment Congress of Dona Ana County filed a lawsuit challenging the approval process, questioning water impacts, oversight, and approval speed.

Microsoft filed plans for two data centers ($350M each) near Castroville while Rowan Digital Infrastructure's Project Cinco is developing a separate $900M hyperscale campus on 440 acres with 300MW capacity. Medina County is in an 'exceptional drought' and the projects would draw water from the Edwards Aquifer. Rowan announced investment in an Edwards Aquifer Authority conservation program to achieve 250% offset of water usage. Eight data centers total are planned for the county, making it a major hotspot. Concerns include traffic, water usage, and environmental impacts near elementary schools.

Infrakey's $10B data center proposal near Elm Mott on 520 acres of farmland would be the largest industrial development in McLennan County history. The facility would include a 1.2GW gas-fired power plant larger than any existing power plant in the county. Opposition has circulated a petition with 3,000+ signatures, created a Facebook group and website, and held regular strategy meetings attended by state legislators including Rep. Pat Curry. Concerns center on air pollution from the gas plant, water consumption, noise, and health impacts. Lacy Lakeview Mayor Charles Wilson defended the project as a 'paradigm-changer' for city finances.

Project Matador, potentially the largest data center on earth (18M sq ft on 6,000 acres), is proposed 12 miles NE of Amarillo. Fermi America entered a letter of intent with Amarillo Utilities to use 2.5-5.5 million gallons/day of potable and municipal graywater. The project threatens the Ogallala Aquifer, the region's primary groundwater supply which is being drained faster than it replenishes. Over 279 comments were filed with TCEQ, mostly opposing, plus 63 contested case hearing requests. Panhandle Taxpayers for Transparency challenged the water agreement. Some residents estimate the area has only five more years of water left. Construction was paused pending TCEQ permits, and a key tenant terminated its agreement in December 2025.

The Page City Council voted 5-2 to sell 500 acres less than one mile from Horseshoe Bend to UK-based Huntley LLC for a $10B, 1GW data center. Residents filed a referendum with 409 hand-signed signatures, but the city rejected it on a technicality. Beth Henshaw gathered over 400 additional signatures to put the issue on a future ballot. Online petitions gathered over 1,800 signatures from a town of only 7,000 people. Lake Powell is approximately 26% full, and residents are deeply concerned about water draw from the Colorado River, which is already stressed and over-allocated.

After Tucson City Council unanimously rejected Project Blue in August 2025 due to vehement community opposition over water usage, Pima County approved the $3.6B data center 3-2. Amazon/AWS was reportedly the end user but backed out around December 2025 after Beale Infrastructure nixed water cooling in favor of air cooling (eliminating direct water consumption). The Arizona Center for Law in the Public Interest sued alleging open meeting law violations. AG Mayes also challenged the deal as unconstitutional rate-setting. Despite having no formal end user and significant opposition, the Pima County Board of Supervisors approved the new data center.

Red Wolf DCD Properties' $12B data center campus (six buildings, 1.8M sq ft, 600MW) on 400 acres in western Wyandotte County is on hold after a protest petition and lawsuit challenged the rezoning. Plaintiffs Neal Palmer and Haskell Farms LLC allege the Unified Government rushed review processes 'without sufficient study of short and long-term impacts.' The lawsuit also claims a commissioner was 'unduly pressured' to recuse himself and that Red Wolf orchestrated efforts to withdraw petition signatures. On October 29, a Wyandotte County district judge denied the government's motion to dismiss. Residents say elected officials have not been transparent about project impacts.

Harvey County Commission unanimously approved a moratorium on data center construction in unincorporated areas through the end of 2028 (roughly three years). Commissioner Don Schroeder explained the moratorium gives the planning department and zoning board time to research and design regulations, as the county currently has no zoning rules for data centers, crypto mining, or related facilities. Concerns centered on water use from the Equus Beds Aquifer. Nearby Andale and Colwich residents pushed back against proposed projects, citing the importance of preserving their farming and ranching community character.

Sedgwick County Commissioners adopted a 90-day pause on data center permit applications (expiring April 17, 2026), with leadership staff proposing extending it to June 11, 2026. Senate majority leader Chase Blasi called for a three-year moratorium, saying 90 days isn't enough. A flood of emails and calls from residents voiced opposition to any data center development. Residents in Colwich and Andale reported hearing about data centers potentially coming less than a mile from homes. Concerns include health issues, noise, environmental impact, traffic, and heavy water use. Town halls were scheduled for March 2026. A proposed 133-mile Evergy transmission line through southern Kansas drew additional opposition from landowners.

Spring Hill City Council voted on October 23, 2025 to move forward with rezoning 316 acres from Rural Residential to M-1 Industrial for a potential data center, though no end user or specific business has been identified. About 300 homes would be impacted, and Spring Hill High School is only 3/4 mile from the proposed site. Approximately 45 people were originally notified about the rezoning. A public hearing was rescheduled to March 25, 2026. Residents are organizing to fight the rezoning, raising concerns about noise, traffic, property values, and proximity to schools.

Luther residents rallied against Beltline Energy's proposed 400-acre data center, citing utility price spikes, water supply impacts, noise, and property values. The Save Oklahoma Farms and Ranches opposition group circulated a petition calling for a moratorium on data center development. The fight is part of a broader wave of data center opposition across Oklahoma.

Beale Infrastructure's $800M Project Atlas proposed 295 acres (235 + 60 acres) for an Amazon/AWS data center with 400MW capacity. The Planning Commission voted 4-1 to deny the rezoning after a 3+ hour meeting. The original plan called for 5.4 million gallons/day water usage before switching to air-cooled design. Despite the denial, the proposal goes to the Coweta city council for a final vote in April 2026.

Google's $9B Project Spring proposed on 827 acres of land annexed by Sand Springs. The Protect Sand Springs Alliance, led by President Kyle Schmidt, raised over $6,000 and gathered 2,058+ petition signatures. Opposition pursued multiple strategies: a nonprofit filed suit in Osage County alleging illegal annexation and ignored community input; residents launched a recall effort gathering 130+ signatures per council member in four hours; and the Osage Nation reviewed the project over concerns about impacts on tribal land. Despite opposition, the Sand Springs City Council approved the rezoning. Concerns include water use, rising utility costs, environmental impacts, noise, and threats to agricultural character.

Laramie County commissioners unanimously approved Project Jade, a 2.7GW data center campus (scalable to 10GW) south of Cheyenne on 1,375 acres. The $50B project by Crusoe Energy and Tallgrass Energy Partners could become the largest US data center. The Hyndman Homesites Homeowners Association wrote a letter detailing concerns about deep wells contaminating the aquifer, increased traffic and noise, light pollution, gas turbine emissions, noxious odors, and wastewater pond locations. Resident Thomas White voiced opposition about noise, light pollution, traffic, and potential water contamination from deep well drilling. The project includes 2.7GW of new natural gas-fired generation.

opposition_groups: Butte Watchdogs for Social and Environmental Justice
updates.summary: Nine nonprofits filed a PSC complaint to stop NorthWestern Energy from shifting data center infrastructure costs to its 413,000+ residential customers. NorthWestern signed Letters of Intent with 11 data center developers for up to 1,400MW total capacity without regulatory approval. The petitioners argue that NorthWestern's plan to sign electricity service agreements before garnering regulatory approval is 'unreasonable, insufficient and contrary to Montana law' and that NorthWestern is inappropriately blocking oversight by shielding letters of intent from public review.

opposition_groups: Butte Watchdogs for Social and Environmental Justice
updates.summary: Sabey Data Centers proposed two 500,000 sq ft buildings (1M sq ft total) on over 600 acres at Montana Connections Industrial Park in Silver Bow County. A coalition of nine nonprofit groups warns the data centers consume massive amounts of water and electricity and could lead to much higher power bills for residents. Opposition cites noise, light pollution, water and power consumption, and potential residential electricity rate increases. Sabey entered an option agreement with the city-county in April, with a February deadline to close.

Hood County (40 miles SW of Fort Worth) has received at least five data center applications. Commissioners voted down a proposed moratorium 3-2, twice, despite 8 hours of public testimony. The 2,100-acre Comanche Circle project sits in a recharge zone for the Upper Trinity Aquifer and could use 1M gallons/day. State Sen. Paul Bettencourt sent a letter to AG Paxton warning that counties lack constitutional authority to impose moratoriums, chilling local opposition. Residents are still adjusting to a nearby bitcoin mining operation in Granbury where neighbors report constant noise affecting people, pets, and wildlife.

Diode Ventures' Gemstone Technology Park received a 3-2 approval (Mayor breaking tie) to rezone 620 acres for a data center requiring 600-800MW of power, equal to about 20% of Idaho Power's current generation capacity. Layne Thornton and Toledo Dairy filed a petition for judicial review against the city, citing traffic impacts on narrow Locust Grove Rd, power and water usage, and precedent for future farmland conversion. The Murray family warned that allowing industrial development 'sets a precedent, and soon the surrounding farmland follows.' Diode Ventures hopes to break ground in 2026 but acknowledged the timeline might be optimistic.

UK-based Eneus Energy plans a 2GW, $14B data center campus on 1,785 acres near Harlingen with 16 data halls. The city-owned utility entered an agreement for 4.6M gallons/day of reclaimed wastewater for the project. Residents addressed the City Commission opposing the development, citing water and electricity concerns in the drought-prone Rio Grande Valley. Cameron County Judge Eddie Trevino said the county's authority was limited since the site is outside city boundaries. Harlingen is drafting an ordinance to cushion data center impacts as companies hunt for sprawling acreage in the area.
